content.text: Agentic AI systems that can read data, write documents, post messages, and send email need a different relationship with human oversight than conversational AI. Cowork is designed around the principle that human oversight is a feature, not a friction — and that the right level of oversight depends on the specific action, not a single global setting.
Human-in-the-loop (HITL)

in Cowork means that humans can be placed at specific decision points in a goal's execution: before an action executes (approval prompts), before a live run starts (sandbox review), or after a goal completes (output review before further use). The goal author chooses where HITL applies based on the risk profile of each action.

This topic covers the philosophy behind HITL in agentic systems, the specific mechanisms Cowork provides, how to choose the right level of oversight for different types of goals, and how to design goals that keep humans appropriately informed without creating review bottlenecks.
Why Human Oversight Matters for Agentic AI

Agentic goals differ from conversational AI in one critical way: they take actions with real-world consequences. A conversational AI that produces a wrong answer can be corrected in the next message. An agentic goal that sends the wrong email to 200 people, archives a critical file, or posts misinformation to a public Slack channel cannot be undone by correction.
The reversibility asymmetry

Most of a goal's reasoning is reversible — the engine can try a different approach if the first one fails. But external write and send operations are largely irreversible. Once an email is sent, it is sent. Once a Slack post is public, it is public. Once a file is deleted, recovery may require backup restoration.
Reasoning errors: recoverable — the engine can try again
Read errors: recoverable — the engine read the wrong data, but can re-read
Write errors to Drive: partially recoverable — file can be deleted or overwritten
Post errors to Slack: partially recoverable — message can be deleted, but recipients may have seen it
Send errors for email: not recoverable — message cannot be unsent from recipient inboxes
Delete errors for Drive files: recoverable only if backup exists or within the service's version history window
The automation vs oversight tradeoff

Full automation is efficient but risky for high-consequence actions. Full supervision eliminates risk but defeats the purpose of automation. The right answer is neither extreme — it is a deliberate choice about where in each goal's execution a human decision point adds value worth the latency cost.

A useful frame: ask "what is the worst plausible outcome if this action is wrong?" If the answer is "we send an embarrassing email to a client" or "we archive a file that turns out to be needed", human review is warranted. If the answer is "we write a summary doc that might have a minor error", auto-approve is fine.
HITL Mechanisms in Cowork
Cowork provides three primary HITL mechanisms, each operating at a different stage of goal execution and suitable for different risk profiles.
1. Sandbox review (before live execution)

Sandbox mode runs the full goal — reads, reasoning, output assembly — without executing any write or send operations. You review the complete proposed output in the sandbox report and decide whether to approve the live run.
Timing: before live execution — you see the full output before any action is taken
Granularity: whole-goal review — you approve or reject the entire run, not individual actions
Overhead: one full goal run in sandbox + review time
Best for: new goals, goals that have changed recently, goals with complex multi-step outputs
2. Approval prompts (during live execution)
Approval prompts pause execution immediately before a specific action and show you the full proposed action for review. You approve, reject, or modify before the engine proceeds.
Timing: at the moment of the action — you see the exact email or Slack post the engine is about to send
Granularity: per-action review — you can approve some actions and reject others in the same goal run
Overhead: pause + human decision time per prompted action
Best for: goals that include external communications, high-visibility posts, or bulk operations
3. Output review (after goal completion)

For goals that produce a structured output (a Drive document, a report, a dataset), a natural HITL point is after the goal completes and before the output is acted on. The goal writes to Drive; a human reviews the document before sending it as an email attachment.
Timing: after goal completion — the output exists but has not been distributed
Granularity: full output review — you read the document before deciding to share it
Overhead: review time for the output document
Best for: research briefs, client-facing reports, legal drafts, any output that will be shared externally
Combining mechanisms

These mechanisms compose. A single goal can use all three: run in sandbox first (sandbox review), then run live with approval prompts enabled for the send actions (approval prompts), then have the output reviewed before being forwarded to a client (output review). Each layer adds overhead but also adds a catchment for different types of errors.
Choosing the Right Oversight Level

The right level of oversight for a goal depends on five factors: the audience for the output, the reversibility of the actions, whether the goal is new or well-tested, whether regulated data is involved, and the blast radius if something is wrong.
Decision guide
Factor
Low oversight appropriate
Higher oversight required
Output audience
Internal team only; not customer-facing
External clients, public channels, or bulk mailing lists
Action reversibility
Drive writes (deletable), draft emails (not sent)
Email sends, Slack posts, file deletions
Goal maturity
10+ sandbox runs, consistent output, no recent changes
New goal, recently changed template, first use with new data
Data sensitivity
General business information, non-regulated
PII, financial data, HIPAA-covered data, confidential IP
Blast radius
Single document, one channel, one recipient
Bulk email to 50+ recipients, public announcement, shared workspace
The trust-but-verify principle

A well-designed HITL strategy starts with full supervision and progressively reduces oversight as a goal demonstrates reliability. This is not about reducing safety — it is about calibrating oversight to the actual risk level based on observed behavior.
New goal: full sandbox + approval prompts for all write actions
After 3–5 sandbox runs with correct output: disable sandbox, keep approval prompts
After 10 live runs with correct approval-prompted output: enable auto-approve for internal actions
After 20+ runs with no issues: enable auto-approve for all actions within the goal's established pattern
Trigger to re-enable oversight: any change to the goal template, any change to the data sources, any change to the output destinations
Human-in-the-Loop in Practice
Pattern: research and review

A common pattern for research goals: the goal runs fully automated for the research and synthesis phase (all read operations, no risk), then pauses for a human to review the draft before it is sent to the intended audience.

Goal structure:
Phase 1 (automated): 
  Read source documents, synthesise findings, write draft to Drive.
  [No approval prompts — writes to Drive are internal and reversible]

Phase 2 (manual human step):
  Human reviews the Drive document and decides whether to distribute.
  If approved, human invokes a second goal: /send-research-brief {{doc_url}} {{recipients}}

Phase 2 goal (approval-gated):
  Email the research brief at {{doc_url}} to {{recipients}}.
  [Approval prompt fires before email send — email is irreversible]

This pattern keeps the automated research phase fully autonomous while maintaining a human gate before the irreversible distribution phase. The two-goal structure makes the gate explicit and separates authoring from distribution.
Pattern: monitor and alert

Scheduled goal (every day at 09:00):
Check the status page at {{url}}.
If any service shows degraded or outage status:
  [Auto-approve: Drive write — log the incident]
  Prompt for approval: Post alert to #ops-incidents in Slack.
  
If all services are operational:
  [Auto-approve: Drive write — log the daily status]
  Do not post to Slack.

This pattern auto-approves low-visibility logging but requires approval before the high-visibility Slack alert. On a good day, the goal runs fully automatically. On a bad day, a human confirms the alert before it goes to the channel — preventing false alarms.
Common HITL Mistakes
Approval theatre

Approval prompts that show too much information and too little context create "approval theatre" — humans approve without meaningfully reviewing the content. A prompt showing a 3000-word email with 50 recipients and a 5-second timer before timeout does not enable genuine review.
Good approval prompts show the critical information first: recipient count, risk level, key message
Full content should be accessible but not required reading for every approval
Approvals should not time out automatically — a pending approval should wait for a human decision
Review fatigue

If every goal requires constant approval and review, teams start rubber-stamping approvals rather than genuinely reviewing them. Calibrate oversight to the actual risk level of each action — not every goal needs every HITL mechanism.
Low-risk internal goals: auto-approve with periodic audit
Medium-risk goals (external audience, bulk): approval prompts for key actions
High-risk goals (regulated data, external communications): sandbox + approval + output review
Reserve the full review stack for goals that genuinely warrant it — not every goal
Treating HITL as an afterthought

The best time to design the HITL strategy for a goal is when authoring it, not after something goes wrong. Every goal that writes to external systems should have a documented answer to: "What happens when this goal produces wrong output, and who catches it?"
Configuring HITL Policies at Workspace Level

Cowork lets workspace administrators define default oversight behaviour across all goals. These workspace-level policies establish the floor that individual goal creators can tighten but cannot loosen — ensuring that organizational risk tolerance is enforced consistently even for goals created by less-experienced users.
Policy Inheritance
Policies flow from workspace → project → goal. A workspace policy of
require_approval: ["external_send", "file_delete"]
means every goal in that workspace must prompt before sending outbound messages or deleting files, regardless of what the goal's own instruction set says.

# workspace_policy.yaml (admin-set)
default_mode: sandbox_first      # all new goals run sandbox unless overridden
require_approval:
  - external_send                # emails, Slack DMs to external users
  - file_delete                  # any Drive file deletion
  - calendar_invite              # calendar events sent to > 2 recipients
auto_approve:
  - drive_read                   # internal read is always safe
  - slack_read                   # reading history never needs approval

# Project-level override (project owner can only restrict, not relax)
# project: Sales Ops
# override:
#   default_mode: auto_run      # NOT permitted — would relax workspace policy
#   require_approval:
#     - external_send           # OK — keeps this restriction
#     - crm_write               # OK — adds a new restriction
Maturity Progression
Teams new to Cowork typically start with the most conservative workspace settings, then relax them as they build trust in specific goal types. A pragmatic progression:
Maturity stage
Default mode
Approval gates
Onboarding (week 1–2)
sandbox_first for all goals
All external actions
Learning (month 1)
sandbox_first for new goal types
External send, delete
Practitioner (month 2+)
auto_run for proven goal types
External send only
Expert team
auto_run with spot review
Batch operations only
Track outcomes at each stage. If a relaxed policy produces a regressions — an unexpected external message, a deleted file — tighten the relevant gate before moving forward.
HITL in Regulated Environments

In regulated industries (finance, healthcare, legal services) HITL is not only a best practice — it may be a compliance requirement. Cowork's oversight mechanisms map directly onto common regulatory control categories.
Audit Trails and Approval Evidence

Every approval decision — human accept, reject, or modify — is recorded in the goal's action log with a timestamp and the identity of the reviewer. This creates an immutable sequence of events suitable for compliance review.

Goal: "quarterly-investor-summary"
Action log excerpt:

2026-04-01T09:12:44Z  sandbox_run  COMPLETE  (draft: 12-page PDF)
2026-04-01T09:13:02Z  approval_prompt  SENT  (reviewer: cfo@acme.com)
2026-04-01T09:28:31Z  approval_decision  ACCEPTED  (reviewer: cfo@acme.com)
  note: "Verified figures against Q1 ledger — approved to send"
2026-04-01T09:28:32Z  external_send  COMPLETE  (recipients: 42 investor contacts)

The CFO's explicit approval and verification note become part of the goal record. In a dispute or audit, this log demonstrates that a qualified human reviewed AI output before it reached external parties.
Four-Eyes Principle for High-Stakes Goals

Some regulatory frameworks require two independent approvers for significant actions. Cowork supports this with a chained approval configuration: the goal pauses for a first reviewer, their decision is logged, and then pauses again for a second independent reviewer before the action executes.
Useful for: large financial transfers, public regulatory filings, M&A documents, mass external communications
Both reviewers see the sandbox output, the first reviewer's notes, and the full goal context
Either reviewer can reject — the action proceeds only after both approve
Rejection by the second reviewer does not automatically re-trigger; the goal pauses for goal-owner intervention
Data Residency and Reviewer Identity

In cross-border deployments, regulatory requirements may restrict which employees can review certain data. Cowork's approval routing can be scoped so that HITL prompts only go to reviewers with the appropriate clearance or geographic jurisdiction — preventing accidental exposure of regulated data to unauthorized personnel during the review step itself.
Accountability When Auto-Approved Goals Go Wrong

Auto-approved goals occasionally produce unintended outcomes — a message sent to the wrong Slack channel, a report based on stale data, a calendar invite sent one day early. Understanding accountability and recovery is as important as setting up oversight in the first place.
The Output Review Window

Even fully auto-approved goals generate an action log that is reviewable after the fact. Establish a routine output review habit: at the end of each week, a team member scans the action logs of high-volume auto-run goals for anomalies. This "asynchronous HITL" catches systematic drift before it becomes a pattern.

Tip: filter action logs by action type external_send and sort by recipient count descending. Goals that sent to unexpectedly large distribution lists are the highest-priority anomalies to investigate.
Recovery Playbook
Identify the erroneous action in the goal's action log (timestamp, action type, payload)
Suspend the goal immediately to prevent recurrence while investigating
Take the manual corrective action (send a correction message, restore the deleted file from backup, cancel the calendar event)
Update the goal's instruction set to add the specific approval gate that would have caught this action
Re-run in sandbox mode to verify the fix, then re-enable with the tighter policy
Document the incident in the goal's description so future maintainers understand why that approval gate exists
Ownership and Responsibility
Auto-approval does not transfer responsibility to the AI. The
goal owner
— the person who created or last modified the goal — is responsible for its outcomes, including outputs produced while the goal ran unattended. Org-wide role clarity:
Role
Responsibility
Goal creator
Accurate instruction set, appropriate scope, correct approval gates
Approver (at prompt)
Verifying the specific action shown before clicking approve
Workspace admin
Setting the floor policy that prevents runaway goals
Team lead
Periodic audit of high-volume auto-run goals for drift
This distributed accountability model ensures that no single failure point — a badly-written instruction, an approver rubber-stamping without reading — brings down a critical workflow.
Summary

Human-in-the-loop in Cowork is a spectrum, not a binary. The right level of oversight depends on the action's reversibility, the audience for the output, the maturity of the goal, and whether regulated data is involved.
Three mechanisms: sandbox review (before live), approval prompts (during live), output review (after live)
Combine mechanisms based on risk — new goals start fully supervised and reduce oversight as they demonstrate reliability
External email sends, public posts, and bulk operations warrant approval prompts regardless of goal maturity
Internal, reversible writes can be auto-approved after thorough sandbox validation
Design the HITL strategy when authoring the goal — not after the first incident
Avoid review fatigue: too many approvals leads to rubber-stamping; calibrate to actual risk
